text.title: DDD Core Patterns
text.summary: Rules and definitions for entities, value objects, aggregates, bounded contexts, and domain services.

rule.statement: Maintain a ubiquitous language glossary in domain/GLOSSARY.md and load it as the first context block of every LLM prompt — single largest quality lever for generated DDD code.
rule.rationale: Without an authoritative glossary, LLMs invent synonyms (User vs Customer vs Account) within the same session, poisoning the codebase across multiple files.

rule.statement: An entity has a stable identity (UUID) that persists over its lifetime. It encapsulates business behavior as methods that enforce invariants, never as naked setters.
rule.rationale: Getters and setters push invariant enforcement to callers, which creates duplication. The entity is the single authority for its own consistency rules.

antipattern.description: Anemic entity: all fields public, all logic in service classes, entity is a bag of data with no behavior.
antipattern.reason: The same invariant ends up enforced in multiple service methods and breaks when one copy is missed.

rule.statement: Value objects are immutable (frozen=True in Python, record in Java), defined by their attributes, and validated in the constructor. Two VOs with equal attributes are interchangeable.
rule.rationale: Mutable VOs break equality semantics and allow illegal states to be introduced after construction. If Money(-5, USD) cannot be constructed, every consumer is automatically safe.

rule.statement: Push validation into the VO or entity constructor/factory method, not into a service.
rule.rationale: Services that validate before creating objects duplicate the rule and leave the window open when called without the guard.

rule.statement: An aggregate is a cluster of entities with one aggregate root. External objects reference the aggregate only through the root, and only by ID — never by direct object pointer to internal entities.
rule.rationale: Object references between aggregates create tight coupling and tempt callers to traverse and mutate internals, breaking the aggregate's consistency boundary.

rule.statement: One aggregate per transaction, always. If two aggregates must change together, publish a domain event from the first and handle it to update the second in a separate transaction.
rule.rationale: Transactions spanning multiple aggregates cause contention, make it hard to reason about consistency, and break the aggregate invariant model.

antipattern.description: Crossing aggregate boundaries: order.customer.wallet.balance accessed directly inside Order.place().
antipattern.reason: Hydrates related aggregates implicitly, creates hidden dependencies, and breaks the aggregate root as the authority for invariants.

rule.statement: A bounded context is an explicit boundary within which a domain model and ubiquitous language are consistent. Different contexts may use the same word for different concepts — translate at the boundary via an Anti-Corruption Layer.
rule.rationale: Without explicit boundaries, model concepts bleed between teams, creating a mudball where Customer in Sales conflicts with Customer in Billing.

rule.statement: Co-locate aggregate, value objects, events, and repository interface in one folder per aggregate (e.g., order/domain/). Forbid imports between sibling aggregate folders.
rule.rationale: Folder boundaries become architectural firewalls. Without them, agents freely import across aggregates.

rule.statement: Dispatch domain events post-commit only (outbox pattern). Collect events on the aggregate during the transaction; drain the buffer after the unit-of-work commits.
rule.rationale: Publishing events before the transaction commits means consumers act on data that may be rolled back, creating phantom side effects (emails sent for orders that never existed).

rule.statement: Wrap every external system whose model you do not control behind an ACL interface defined in the domain. Translate in the infrastructure implementation, never inside the domain.
rule.rationale: Letting external API terminology leak into the domain contaminates the ubiquitous language and couples business rules to third-party contracts.
